title: Royal icing in three piping consistencies
subtitle: Stiff for piping, medium for flooding, runny for spreading
excerpt: A technique tutorial for royal icing in the three working consistencies — stiff for outline-piping, medium for flooding sections, and runny for filling between. Made from a single batch of icing, water-thinned in stages. Covers consistency tests, piping bag fill, and recovery from over-thinning.
sourceNotes: Royal icing (icing sugar beaten with egg white and lemon) is documented in Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 as a household standard for piped and flooded cake decoration. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): documents the British wedding-cake tradition. The three-consistency system (stiff / medium / runny) is the modern cake-decorating approach developed in early-twentieth-century baking schools.

recipe.yieldDescription: 300 g icing — enough to decorate 24 biscuits
recipe.freezeNotes: Royal icing keeps in an airtight container in the fridge for 3 days; covered with cling film touching the surface to stop a crust forming.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make a batch ahead, store covered with cling film touching the surface to prevent a crust.

recipeTools.notes: Or a hand mixer; a wooden spoon takes longer.
Round PME #2 (1.5 mm) for outline; round PME #3 (2 mm) for flood; large round PME #4 for runny.

glossaryTerms.definition: The standard medium-consistency test for royal icing: drag a knife through the icing in the bowl. The mark left by the knife should disappear in about 15 seconds. Faster than 15 (in 5–10 seconds) means a thin runny consistency. Slower than 15 (still visible at 30 seconds) means a thick stiff consistency. Fifteen-second icing flows smoothly into outlined shapes without escaping the lines or leaving bumps.

The standard royal-icing decorating method on biscuits. Stiff icing is piped in an outline around the perimeter of the biscuit; the outline acts as a dam. Medium 'flood' icing is then piped inside the outline to fill the surface; the icing self-levels into a smooth top within seconds. Both icings are the same recipe, thinned with different amounts of water.

content.text: Royal icing is the canonical biscuit and cake-decorating icing: icing sugar beaten with egg whites and a little lemon juice to a smooth, pure-white paste. The same batch is thinned with water in stages to produce three working consistencies — stiff for outline-piping, medium for flooding sections, runny for filling between thin lines.

The consistency test is precise: a fifteen-second mark left by a knife through the icing. Hold the bowl to one side and assess each pour by eye; the icing thickens as it sits in the bag, so adjust as you go.

items.prepNote: from about 3 large eggs; or use 60 g of meringue powder mixed with 90 ml water as a pasteurised alternative
for thinning, added by the teaspoon as needed

content.text: Make the base icing — stiff consistency

Sift the {{icing-sugar}} into the bowl of a stand mixer. Add the {{egg-whites}} and juice of the {{lemon}}. Beat on low until incorporated, then on medium for 5 minutes until the icing is glossy, pure white, and holds stiff peaks when the whisk is lifted. This is the stiff-consistency icing — for piping outlines on biscuits and for fine decorative lines. Cover any portion you are not using right away with a damp cloth or cling film touching the surface.

Spoon half the stiff icing into a second bowl. Add the {{water}} a teaspoon at a time, stirring with a silicone spatula. After each teaspoon, test by dragging a knife through the icing; you want the mark to disappear in about 15 seconds. This is medium 'flood' icing — for filling outlined shapes on biscuits and for cake-board work.

Take a third bowl, spoon in some medium icing, and add another teaspoon of water — the runny consistency leaves a mark that disappears in 5 seconds. Use it for runouts and for filling between very fine piped lines. To use: pipe the stiff icing around the perimeter of a biscuit as an outline; immediately pipe the medium icing inside the outline to flood; use a cocktail stick to coax it into corners. Let the flooded biscuit dry for 30 minutes before piping any detail on top.

If the icing is too thin, beat in a tablespoon of sifted icing sugar at a time until the test gives the right mark. If the surface dries crackled rather than smooth, the icing was too thin; thicken with icing sugar and try again. If the icing tears or pulls into ridges as it flows, it is too thick; thin with a few drops of water. The consistency is the recipe; the precision is in the test.
